Problem Statement: For the following exercises, consider the function $$f ( x ) = \frac{x^{2} - 1}{| x - 1 |} .$$
Question:
[T] Complete the following table for the function. Round your solutions to four decimal places. \begin{table}[h]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
x & $$f ( x )$$ &  & x & $$f ( x )$$ \\ \hline
0.9 & a. &  & 1.1 & e. \\ \hline
0.99 & b. & 1.01 & f. \\ \hline
0.999 & c. & 1.001 & g. \\ \hline
0.9999 & d. & 1.0001 & h. \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, consider the function $$f ( x ) = \frac{x^{2} - 1}{| x - 1 |} .$$
Question: What do your results in the preceding exercise indicate about the two-sided limit $$\underset{x \rightarrow 1}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) ?$$ Explain your response.
Answer: $$\underset{x \rightarrow 1}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right)$$ does not exist because $$\underset{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = -2 \neq \underset{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 2 .$$

Problem Statement: [T] In the following exercises, set up a table of values and round to eight significant digits. Based on the table of values, make a guess about what the limit is. Question:
$$\underset{\theta \rightarrow 0}{\text{lim}} \text{sin} \left(\right. \frac{\pi}{\theta} \left.\right)$$ \begin{table}[h]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
θ & $$\text{sin} ( \frac{\pi}{\theta} )$$ &  & θ & $$\text{sin} ( \frac{\pi}{\theta} )$$ \\ \hline
-0.1 & a. &  & 0.1 & e. \\ \hline
-0.01 & b. & 0.01 & f. \\ \hline
-0.001 & c. & 0.001 & g. \\ \hline
-0.0001 & d. & 0.0001 & h. \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Problem Statement: [T] In the following exercises, set up a table of values and round to eight significant digits. Based on the table of values, make a guess about what the limit is. Then, use a calculator to graph the function and determine the limit. Was the conjecture correct? If not, why does the method of tables fail?
Answer: a. 10.00000; b. 100.00000; c. 1000.0000; d. 10,000.000; Guess: $$\underset{\alpha \rightarrow 0^{+}}{\text{lim}} \frac{1}{\alpha} \text{cos} \left(\right. \frac{\pi}{\alpha} \left.\right) = \infty ,$$ actual: DNE

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, consider the graph of the function $$y = f ( x )$$ shown here. Which of the statements about $$y = f ( x )$$ are true and which are false? Explain why a statement is false.
Question: $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 5$$
Answer: False; $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right)$$ DNE since $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6^{-}}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 2$$ and $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6^{+}}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 5 .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, sketch the graph of a function with the given properties.
Question: Shock waves arise in many physical applications, ranging from supernovas to detonation waves. A graph of the density of a shock wave with respect to distance, x , is shown here. We are mainly interested in the location of the front of the shock, labeled $$x_{\text{SF}}$$ in the diagram. Evaluate $$\underset{x \rightarrow x_{S F} ^{+}}{\text{lim}} \rho \left(\right. x \left.\right) .$$ Evaluate $$\underset{x \rightarrow x_{S F} ^{-}}{\text{lim}} \rho \left(\right. x \left.\right) .$$ Evaluate $$\underset{x \rightarrow x_{S F}}{\text{lim}} \rho \left(\right. x \left.\right) .$$ Explain the physical meanings behind your answers.
Answer: a. $$\rho_{2}$$ b. $$\rho_{1}$$ c. DNE unless $$\rho_{1} = \rho_{2} .$$ As you approach $$x_{\text{SF}}$$ from the left, you are in the high-density area of the shock. When you approach from the right, you have not experienced the “shock” yet and are at a lower density.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, sketch the graph of a function with the given properties.
Question:
A track coach uses a camera with a fast shutter to estimate the position of a runner with respect to time. A table of the values of position of the athlete versus time is given here, where x is the position in meters of the runner and t is time in seconds. What is $$\underset{t \rightarrow 2}{\text{lim}} x \left(\right. t \left.\right) ?$$ What does it mean physically? \begin{table}[h]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline
t(sec) & x(m) \\ \hline
1.75 & 4.5 \\ \hline
1.95 & 6.1 \\ \hline
1.99 & 6.42 \\ \hline
2.01 & 6.58 \\ \hline
2.05 & 6.9 \\ \hline
2.25 & 8.5 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
